steps.title: Use what the added value does
steps.content: The new data set adds one salmon weight, $68$ pounds, to the original $14$ weights. From the histogram, $68$ pounds is greater than every original weight, so it is also greater than the original mean.

steps.title: Check the median
steps.content:
Track the middle positions:
  • With $14$ original values, the median is the average of the $7$th and $8$th values.
  • After adding $68$ pounds at the high end, the new data set has $15$ values, so the median is the $8$th value.
  • The median would be greater only if the $8$th value were greater than the average of the $7$th and $8$th values.
The histogram does not force that; the $7$th and $8$th values could be equal. So the median is not guaranteed to increase.

steps.title: Check the mean
steps.content: Adding a value above the current mean pulls the mean upward. Since $68$ pounds is above every original weight, it is definitely above the original mean. Therefore, the mean must increase.

steps.title: Confirm the choice
steps.content:
• I: Not guaranteed. The median could stay the same.
  • II: Guaranteed. The mean must increase.
Only Statement II must be greater, so B is correct.
